title: ūüďļ‚ú® Learn Korean Phrases with K-Drama: Ūóź! [heol] (slang)
description:
Today‚Äôs phrase is "Ūóź", a popular reaction word in Korean dramas used when someone is shocked, surprised, or at a loss for words! It‚Äôs a super casual expression perfect for those moments when you‚Äôre just... speechless. ūü§Į

In English, "Ūóź" can be translated as:

- OMG!
- No way!
- What?!
- Seriously?

Enjoy learning this fun expression with real K-drama moments! ūüźįūü§ó

title: Learn Hangul in 90 Minutes - Start to Finish [Complete Series]
description:
Learn Hangul quickly in only 90 minutes. Hangul is the Korean alphabet, and this video will cover everything you need to know from its history, to how to read and write it, and sound change rules for proper pronunciation. Get something to write with and start learning Korean the right way - from Hangul.

Want to start learning Korean? To take the next step, check out my book, "Korean Made Simple" on Amazon: http://amzn.to/2bDBi6h (affiliate)

This video is a compilation of the videos from this playlist: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLbFrQnW0BNMVrHEJCOYWbLnXFzj4bl5z0

Why did I put all of these videos together? For people who just want to watch 1 video and be done with the whole thing. If you have more time, check out the videos individually in the playlist.

Wondering what to do after you've learned Hangul? Check out this guide I made: https://youtu.be/WGoj1V6qKTw

5 Best Podcasts for Korean Learners
description:
Learning Korean with podcasts can be a fun and effective way to improve your language skills. Not only do they help improve listening skills but also provide access to native speakers and a range of accents and dialects.

So, here are 5 best podcasts to listen to.

5 . Cozy Storytime in Korean
Length: 4 - 7 minutes
Level: Beginner and up¬†
Best for improving listening skills. Teaches about Korean culture and many more everyday things in Korean through bedtime stories.

4. KingKa: A K Drama Podcast
Length: 30 - 60 minutes
Level: Beginner and up
Uses K-drama as a platform to discuss elements of culture and language.

3. Intermediate Korean
Length: 5 - 15 minutes
Level: Intermediate / Advanced
Uses natural language and stories to teach listeners important linguistic concepts and grammar as well as elements of Korean culture and history.

2. Breathe in Korean
Length: 10 - 20 minutes
Level: Intermediate / Advanced
It has inspiring quotations from various literary works in Korean and English. It's both informative and motivating, as well as relaxing. For those who need more exposure and listening practice.
¬†¬†

1. Talk to Me in Korean
Length:¬† 3 - 20 minutes
Level: All Levels
Lesson content is interesting and engaging, with episodes varying in length and difficulty. This means there truly is something for everyone and if you're looking for a podcast that will teach you the Korean language well without going too in-depth into cultural elements or informal language, then you have found one of the best.

title: ūüďļLearn Korean Phrases with K-Drama: ‚ÄĚI‚Äôll do it‚Äú #kdrama #learningkorean #realkorean #korean
description:
Today‚Äôs expression is ‚ÄĚŽāīÍįÄ Ūē†Í≤Ć,‚Äú meaning ‚ÄĚI‚Äôll do it‚Äú or ‚ÄĚI‚Äôll take care of it.‚Äú This phrase is commonly used when volunteering to do something or taking responsibility for a task. You can use it in casual situations among friends or close peers.

ūüďĚ Breakdown
ŽāīÍįÄ [nae¬∑ga]: I (subject, casual)
ŪēėŽč§ [ha¬∑da]: to do
Ūē†Í≤Ć [hal¬∑ge]: a form meaning ‚ÄĚI will do‚Äú

In polite language, you can say ‚ÄĚž†úÍįÄ Ūē†Í≤ĆžöĒ‚Äú [je¬∑ga hal¬∑kke¬∑yo], where ‚ÄĚž†úÍįÄ‚Äú is a more respectful form of ‚ÄĚI,‚Äú and ‚ÄĚ-žöĒ‚Äú adds a polite ending.

Use this phrase to show helpfulness or readiness to act! ūüėä

title: 50 Essential Korean Words Every Beginner Must Know - Learn Korean with your favorite Kdramas!
description:
In this video you can learn 50 very common and useful Korean words with examples from many different Korean dramas. Note that most of these words and expressions are informal and would normally only be used with people you are familiar with like friends or family, but they are nevertheless used very often and you will hear them frequently in Korean.
Also be aware that Korean and English are very different languages and it is not always easy to directly translate the meaning or pronunciation of a Korean word. 

For example, in Korean the "L" is often pronounced closer to an English rolling "R".
Also, when a Korean word starts with an "M" as in for instance [moleugesseo], the "M" is denasalized in Korean making it sound closer to an English "B". It's comparable to saying "moleugesseo" while pinching your nose or having a cold!
Similarly, the "N" at the beginning of Korean words often sounds closer to an English "D", as in for instance [naeil].


Check out my channel for lots more! :)

description:

Hey guys! I made a Korean tutorial teaching you how to read Korean. It is very easy compared to English, to be honest, and I made sure to make this tutorial incredibly detailed with step-by-step processes. You will be able to follow along with the video as you're watching.

title: Let‚Äôs Practice Speaking Korean Together! ūüĒ• 3-Step Training: Listen, Speak & Break It Down
description:
ūüďĆ Useful Tips (not mentioned in the video)

1. Catching Rabbits? ūüźá
‚Üí This isn‚Äôt something I made up, and it's a real Korean expression! ūüėĀ In Korean, instead of "catching birds" as in the English phrase "catch two birds with one stone," we often say "catching rabbits." ūüźá Of course, we do use the hanja proverb žĚľžĄĚžĚīž°į (šłÄÁü≥šļĆť≥•) that means "one(žĚľ) stone(žĄĚ), two(žĚī) birds(ž°į)," but in native Korean, ŪÜ†ŽĀľ (rabbits) is more frequently used.

2. Speech Levels in the Conversation
‚Üí Both the professor and student use ž°īŽĆďŽßź (polite speech) in this conversation. But if you listen closely, you‚Äôll notice that the student speaks more respectfully and uses honorific verbs like -(žúľ)žčúŽč§ and ŽďúŽ¶¨Žč§ often. In contrast, the professor never uses these forms. When creating this dialogue, I imagined about a 20-year age gap between them. In real life, it would sound unnatural for someone much older, like the professor, to use overly formal speech with a student. (This shows how, even within the same ž°īŽĆďŽßź, the use or omission of honorific verbs can make the speech sound simpler or more respectful.)

ūüĒó If you‚Äôd like to learn more about honorific verbs, you can watch this class: https://youtu.be/v7oFvmlrKD8?si=BBTNXLiw6gl7Tj1W

3. Is This Video Too Difficult for Beginners?
‚Üí This content is ideal for intermediate learners, but even beginners can benefit! If you picked up just a few phrases or vocabulary, that‚Äôs a great start! ūüėä

This class is for learners who are just starting Korean grammar! ūüďĚ

We‚Äôll start from the very basics ‚ÄĒ like simple sentence structure and the core particles žĚÄ/ŽäĒ, žĚī/ÍįÄ and žĚĄ/Ž•ľ, ‚ÄĒ and I‚Äôll explain them in a way that‚Äôs easy to understand even if you‚Äôre just getting started. We‚Äôll also practice making simple sentences together.

It‚Äôs recommended that you‚Äôre already somewhat familiar with Hangeul, the Korean alphabet; you don‚Äôt need to be 100% confident yet, but having a basic understanding of Korean consonants, vowels, and batchim will help. If you‚Äôve never learned Hangeul at all, it might be a bit difficult to follow.

I'm excited about this class!ūüôĆūüŹĽ‚ú®

title: Korean Grammar & Speaking Practice: -„ĄĻ/žĚĄ ŪÖźŽćį, ŪÖĆŽčąÍĻĆ (What's this "ŪÖĆ"?)
description:
-„ĄĻ/žĚĄ ŪÖźŽćį and -„ĄĻ/žĚĄ ŪÖĆŽčąÍĻĆ are among the most frequently asked questions I get from intermediate students. Honestly, organizing the usage of -„ĄĻ/žĚĄ ŪÖĆ was a bit of a headache because it wasn't easy to come up with a definitive grammatical explanation for this. That‚Äôs why, instead of focusing on what‚Äôs absolutely correct from a grammatical perspective, I prioritized how these expressions are actually used in real-life conversations, like what‚Äôs more commonly heard in everyday speech. This video reflects the most common and practical usage of these expressions in modern spoken Korean :) So please keep this in mind as you learn!

This lesson would be difficult to follow if you haven't taken the basic ŪēúÍłÄ (Hangul, Korean alphabets) lesson yet. In that case, you can start your first hangul lesson here:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=85qJXvyFrIc

ŽįõžĻ® (batchim, aka the final consonant) is part of hangul that confuses learners the most. So I made separate lessons (part1, part2, part 3 - the other two will be up as well) to explain more in-depth about ŽįõžĻ®. It's a long lesson because I've included a lot of read-it-together practice. If you find it too long, you can always speed up the video or skip to only the parts you need help with.

I'll see you in the next part 2 video (the link is at the top). We'll learn more about žěźžĚĆŽŹôŪôĒ (consonant assimilation) a little of which we've also covered in this vid at „ÖéŽįõžĻ® section and at the very last bit.

Video Description

ŽįõžĻ® Part 2 - Here we learn how to read words and sentences where consonant assimilation (žěźžĚĆŽŹôŪôĒ) happens! I know it gets trickier to understand in some parts, but as I always say, with more encounter and practice with various words and sentences, you'll get used to it and you'll also get to a level where you automatically make the correct pronunciation without even thinking.  Of course, that doesn't happen overnight, so I suggest you keep studying words and practice how to read them. Making sound while memorizing will help the words stick in your head a lot longer than otherwise as well :)

title: žĚÄ/ŽäĒ & žĚī/ÍįÄ (Subject Marking Particles) Explained
description:
When to use žĚÄ/ŽäĒ and žĚī/ÍįÄ is one of what Korean learners find the most confusing to understand. Native Korean speakers can easily tell the difference, because we‚Äôve grown up in this language our entire life, but we find it very difficult to explain this to non-natives. There are numerous contexts when using a language and it would be impossible to verbalize all of them, so I doubt there can be one single, clear answer to žĚÄ/ŽäĒ/žĚī/ÍįÄ confusion. But I do hope that the explanation and example sentences I have for you in this lesson help you inch closer to getting it. ŪôĒžĚīŪĆÖ, everyone! ūüí™

(If you want to skip right on to žĚÄ/ŽäĒ & žĚī/ÍįÄ comparison, you can start from 08:27 If you want to know what a particle and a subject marker is in Korean language, you're welcome to stay with me all the way from the beginning ūüėä)

description:
How can you reach intermediate level in Korean in a relatively short amount of time? It‚Äôs simple. Read a lot, listen a lot, write a lot, speak a lot. But I bet you already know that. So, instead of that, I‚Äôm going to tell you an easy and effective way to shorten time for you to reach intermediate level.

What I want to recommend you the most as a Korean learning method is to learn Korean sentence patterns. Here‚Äôs why.

By learning Korean sentence patterns, even for absolute beginners, it‚Äôs possible to speak complicated sentences by just changing words!

title: 1st~20th, 100th, Korean ordinal numbers, Korean language #shorts
description:
Let‚Äôs learn Korean ordinal numbers!
You need to use the native Korean number system rather than the Sino-Korean number system, but of course you need to modify the numbers.
in English, most ordinal numbers end with ~th.
How about Korean ordinal numbers?
Let‚Äôs find out!

title: Talking about Your Day #1 | 10 Short Korean Conversations | Beginner Korean | A1-A2-B1
description:
Boost your Korean listening skills with 10 realistic daily life conversations! ‚ú® 

In this video, we cover common scenarios like meeting friends at a cafe, shopping, and chatting at the library. Each conversation is played at two speeds: Slow (for clear pronunciation) and Normal (for natural flow). Perfect for Beginner to Intermediate learners (A1-A2-B1).

ūüďĚ Personal Note: 
Drafting these 10 conversations was quite a nostalgic process for me! I tried to recall the specific struggles I had when I first started speaking Korean‚ÄĒlike what to say when running into a friend unexpectedly or how to make small talk about a tiring day. I focused on including the natural phrases I wish I had known back then. I hope you find them helpful and relatable!

‚ö†ÔłŹ Disclaimer:
The content presented in this video is fictional and created solely for educational purposes. Please note that AI technology was used to assist in the production of this video to ensure high-quality learning material.

title: [Korean Pronunciation] Length of Sound, Intonation, Stress & Pause Reading
description:
Only the length of sound is mentioned in the standard language rules of modern Korean. Korean is not the language with strong intonation and stress, but the intention or expressed emotions of the speaker would be different depending on the intonation and stress. But it's important to practice the meaning-centered pause reading in order to understand where you put stress and how you make an intonation.

From Billie Eilish‚Äôs breathy vocals in What Was I Made For? to Gracie Abrams‚Äô soft whispers in I Miss You, I‚Äôm Sorry, today‚Äôs artists are intentionally changing the way they sing. But why? title: One-Stop Guide to Korean Particles - žĚÄ/ŽäĒ/žĚī/ÍįÄ (Subject & Topic Markers)
description:
If you're one of the millions of Korean learners who have struggled to understand exactly how the Korean particles žĚÄ, ŽäĒ, žĚī and ÍįÄ work, this will be a great one-stop guide for you! People who learn Korean as their mother tongue can distinguish their specific roles and various usages at a very young age, but for learners who study Korean as a foreign language, these topic/subject markers can be a bit tricky to understand. 

Here's the gist of this video: 
- žĚÄ and ŽäĒ are "topic markers" and show you what the topic of the sentence is. 
- žĚī and ÍįÄ are "subject markers" and show you the direct subject of the verb that is being used in the sentence.
- žĚÄ/ŽäĒ also shows contrast and change of scene/narrative. 
- In compound sentences, such as "The movie I watched was fun.", you can see žĚÄ/ŽäĒ and žĚī/ÍįÄ being used together. 

But for a better understanding, we recommend that you watch the full video (more than once if necessary) and pay close attention to the detailed examples.

 ----------------------------------

Names of Chess Pieces
description:
King - The most important piece on the board, the king can move one square in any direction.

Queen - The most powerful piece, the queen can move diagonally, horizontally, or vertically any number of squares.

Rook - Also known as a castle, the rook can move horizontally or vertically any number of squares.

Bishop - The bishop moves diagonally any number of squares.

Knight - The knight moves in an L-shape, two squares in one direction and then one square perpendicular to that.

Pawn - The least powerful piece, the pawn can only move forward one square at a time, except for on its first move when it can move two squares forward. Pawns capture diagonally one square forward. Pawns can also promote to another piece if they reach the opposite end of the board.

title: 11 Typical Korean Expressions - Learn Korean with Kdramas!
description:
In this video you will learn 11 very common Korean expressions or exclamations. Most of these are versatile words that can't be translated in a single way but can have multiple meanings depending on the situation or the way they are said. This makes them very useful, but also more difficult to understand for Korean learners, which is why I've added some example situations for every word to show you how they are used.

The words covered in this video are:

Ūóź [heol] meaning "Wow / OMG / No way!"
žĄ§Žßą [seolma] meaning "No way / Surely not / Don't tell me..."
žĖīŽ®ł [eomeo] meaning "Oh! / Oh my / Oh my goodness"
ŪėĻžčú [hoksi] meaning "Perhaps / By any chance"
žó≠žčú [yeoksi] meaning "As expected / As I thought / I knew it"
ŽĆÄŽįē [daebak] meaning "Wow / Amazing / Awesome"
ŪēėÍłī [hagin] meaning "I guess / I suppose / That figures"
Í∑łŽÉ• [geunyang] meaning "Just / Just because / It's just that / No reason"
žĚłž†ē [injeong] meaning "(I) admit / agree / acknowledge"
ÍĪįŽīź [geobwa] meaning "See? (I told you)"
žēĄžĚīÍ≥† / žēĄžĚīÍĶ¨ [aigo / aigu] meaning "Oh! / Oh dear / Oh no / Gosh"

title: Top 10 Super Easy Korean words - Learn Korean with your favorite Kdramas!
description:
In this video you can learn 10 very easy common words in Korean.
In Korean the level of politeness in your speech is important, depending on who you are talking to. These 10 words are mostly for informal situations, for example with friends, family, or people of similar age as you.

The 10 words covered in this video are:
Hey! - written in Korean as žēľ and pronounced as [ya]
You - ŽĄą pronounced as [neo]. Note that the N at the beginning of a Korean word is often denasalized, making it sound closer to an English D. It's similar to saying "neo" while having a cold or pinching your nose.
Excuse me - ž†ÄÍłį pronounced as [jeogi], it is used to get someone's attention
Sorry - ŽĮłžēą pronounced as [mian]. Note that similar to the N, the M at the beginning of Korean words is often denasalized, making it sound closer to an English B. It's similar to saying "mian" while having a cold or pinching your nose.
Hi/Bye - žēąŽÖē pronounced as [annyeong]. A common way to informally say Hi when meeting someone, but can also be said when leaving.
Thanks - Í≥†ŽßąžõĆ pronounced as [gomawo]
Fine/I like it - žĘčžēĄ pronounced as [joh-a], it is often used when you agree with someone's suggestion, but can also be used to say that you like something (or someone).
I know - žēĆžēĄ pronounced as [ala], note that the L sounds closer to a rolling R, though it is not exactly an English L or R, but somewhere in between.
Okay/Sure - Í∑łŽěė pronounced as [geulae]
Go/Bye - ÍįÄ pronounced as [ga], often used when saying goodbye to someone. It might sound strange to simply say "Go" to someone leaving, but in Korean this is quite normal and has no negative connotation, it can be compared to saying "Take care" in English.

title: ūüďļ‚ú® Learn Korean Phrases with K-Drama: "What are they saying?" or "What did they say?"
description:
Today's expression is "Ž≠źŽěė?" (casual) and "Ž≠źŽěėžöĒ?" (polite), which literally means "What are they saying?" or "What did they say?"

In conversation, Ž≠źŽěėžöĒ? is usually used to ask what someone said in a neutral or polite way. However, Ž≠źŽěė? is often used with a sarcastic or dismissive tone when reacting to something that sounds ridiculous, unreasonable, or hard to believe. In these situations, it can be understood as "What's that supposed to mean?", "What are they even talking about?", or "What nonsense are they saying?"

Because of this nuance, the sarcastic meaning is much more common with the casual form Ž≠źŽěė? than with the polite form Ž≠źŽěėžöĒ?.



ūüďĚ Breakdown

Ž≠ź [mwo]: what
-Žěė [-rae]: contracted form related to -ŽĚľÍ≥† Ūēī, meaning "someone said" or "they say"

title: Koreans ūüáįūüá∑watching kdrama Vs Indians ūüáģūüá≥watching kdrama #funnyshorts #ytshorts #kdrama
50 Essential Korean Questions Every Beginner Must Know - Learn Korean with your favorite Kdramas!
description:
In this video you can learn 50 very common and useful Korean questions with examples from many different Korean dramas. Note that most of these words and expressions are informal and would normally only be used with friends or family, but they are nevertheless used very often and you will hear them frequently in Korean. Also note that Korean and English are very different languages and it is not always easy to directly translate the meaning or pronunciation of a Korean word. 

For example, in Korean the "L" is often pronounced closer to an English rolling "R".
Also, when a Korean word starts with an "M" as in for instance [mwoya], the "M" is denasalized in Korean making it sound closer to an English "B". It's comparable to saying "mwoya" while pinching your nose or having a cold!
Similarly, the "N" at the beginning of Korean words often sounds closer to an English "D", as in for instance [nugu].

title: ūüďļLearn Korean Phrases with K-Drama: 7 Most Commonly Used Words by Koreans
description:
These 7 expressions were picked as some of the most frequently used phrases in everyday Korean conversation. You‚Äôll hear them not only in K-dramas but also in daily chats all over Korea. Learn these to sound more natural and emotionally expressive in Korean!


ūüďĚ Breakdown

ūüĎČ žēĄŽčą [a¬∑ni]
Literally "no," but often used as a filler to start a sentence, especially when expressing surprise, frustration, or disbelief. It doesn't actually mean "no" in these cases ‚ÄĒ think of it like "Wait," "Seriously," or "Come on" in English.

ūüĎČ Í∑ľŽćį [geun¬∑de]
Short for "Í∑łŽüįŽćį," meaning "but" or "by the way." It‚Äôs used to smoothly contrast what was said before or to shift the topic. A very common way to connect thoughts in casual conversation.

ūüĎČ žěąžěĖžēĄ [it¬∑jja¬∑na]
Used when trying to get someone's attention or start explaining something, similar to "You know..." or "So..." in English. It often signals that the speaker wants to say something important or personal.

ūüĎČ žÜĒžßĀŪěą [sol¬∑jji¬∑ki]
Means "honestly" or "to be frank." Used when you‚Äôre about to share your true thoughts or feelings. Often adds sincerity or a blunt tone to what follows.

ūüĎČ žßĄžßú [jin¬∑jja]
Literally means "really," but it's also used for strong emphasis. Depending on tone, it can show excitement, annoyance, disbelief, or seriousness ‚ÄĒ much like "Seriously" or "For real" in English.

ūüĎČ žĚłÍįĄž†ĀžúľŽ°ú [in¬∑gan¬∑jeo¬∑geu¬∑ro]
Literally "as a human" or "humanly speaking," but often used as a filler to emphasize a point or appeal to someone's decency. In English, it's closer to "Seriously," "Come on," or "Be reasonable."

ūüĎČ žēĹÍįĄ [yak¬∑kkan]
Means "a bit" or "kind of." While it can describe small amounts, it's also commonly used as a filler to soften statements or make things sound less direct, much like "sort of" or "kinda" in English.


ūüé¨ These everyday expressions show up constantly in K-dramas and real-life Korean conversations. Learn them, and you‚Äôll instantly sound more natural and expressive in Korean! ūüáįūüá∑ūüó£ÔłŹ

title: 10 Most Common Korean Phrases in K-Dramas
description:
10 Most Common Korean Phrases in K-Dramas:‚Ā†
‚Ā†
1. žēąŽÖēŪēėžĄłžöĒ (Annyeonghaseyo) ‚ÄĒ Hello / Hi‚Ā†
This is the standard polite way to say hello in Korean. You‚Äôll hear it almost everywhere in dramas, whether characters are meeting for the first time or greeting friends politely.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
2. žā¨ŽěĎŪēīžöĒ (Saranghaeyo) ‚ÄĒ I love you‚Ā†
The classic phrase to confess your love! You‚Äôll hear this in the most romantic moments ‚ÄĒ usually said by the male lead, but anyone can use it.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
3. Í≥†ŽßąžõĆžöĒ (Gomawoyo) ‚ÄĒ Thank you‚Ā†
A polite way to say thanks. It‚Äôs less formal than Í≥†ŽßôžäĶŽčąŽč§ (gomapseumnida) but very common in daily conversations and dramas.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
4. ŽĮłžēąŪēīžöĒ (Mianhaeyo) ‚ÄĒ Sorry‚Ā†
Used to apologize gently. You‚Äôll often hear this when characters feel regret or want to soften a tough conversation.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
5. žßĄžßú? (Jinjja?) ‚ÄĒ Really?‚Ā†
A fun, casual expression to show surprise or disbelief. Perfect for reacting to shocking or unexpected news!‚Ā†
‚Ā†
6. ŽĆÄŽįē (Daebak) ‚ÄĒ Awesome / Amazing / Wow‚Ā†
A slang term that literally means ‚Äúbig hit‚ÄĚ or ‚Äújackpot,‚ÄĚ but used like ‚ÄúWow!‚ÄĚ or ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs amazing!‚ÄĚ It‚Äôs super common among younger characters.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
7. žôú Í∑łŽěė? (Wae geurae?) ‚ÄĒ What‚Äôs wrong? / Why are you like that?‚Ā†
A caring or concerned question often asked when someone notices a friend or loved one acting strange.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
8. ÍīúžįģžēĄžöĒ (Gwaenchana-yo) ‚ÄĒ It‚Äôs okay / I‚Äôm okay‚Ā†
A reassuring phrase that means ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs fine‚ÄĚ or ‚ÄúI‚Äôm alright.‚ÄĚ It‚Äôs comforting and often used after someone apologizes or when calming someone down.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
9. žßĄžč¨žĚīžēľ (Jinsimiya) ‚ÄĒ I‚Äôm serious / I mean it‚Ā†
Used to emphasize sincerity, especially when making important statements or confessions.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
10. ŪěėŽāī! (Himnae!) ‚ÄĒ Cheer up! / Stay strong!‚Ā†
A supportive phrase used to encourage someone going through a hard time. You‚Äôll often see friends or lovers say this to lift spirits.‚Ā†

title: ūüďļLearn Korean Phrases with K-Drama: "Just a moment"
description:
Today's expression is "žě†žčúŽßĆ" or its polite form "žě†žčúŽßĆžöĒ," which means "Just a moment" or "Excuse me for a second" in English. This phrase is used when you want someone to wait for a short moment, or when you're trying to politely get someone's attention or navigate through a crowd.

ūüďĚ Breakdown  
žě†žčú [jam¬∑si]: a short moment  
ŽßĆ [man]: only  

Polite form: žě†žčúŽßĆžöĒ  

You‚Äôll hear this often in public places, like when someone tries to pass through or when asking someone to pause or wait briefly. It's a super useful expression for daily life in Korea!


ūüĎČ Difference between žě†žčúŽßĆ and žě†ÍĻźŽßĆ

These two are often used interchangeably in casual conversations, but there‚Äôs a slight nuance difference!

- žě†žčúŽßĆ feels slightly more formal and can imply a bit longer pause. You might hear this more in service settings (like when a staff member asks you to wait).
- žě†ÍĻźŽßĆ sounds more casual and is often used among friends or when you‚Äôre asking someone to wait just a tiny moment.


I hope this video is interesting and helpful to you! ūüźįūü§ó

title: 50 More Super Useful Korean Words Every Beginner Must Know - Learn Korean with your favorite Kdramas
description:
Link to my first 50 Korean Words video: https://youtu.be/RHyj1MG898Q
Link to my 50 Korean Questions video: https://youtu.be/CWaIEaswdRg

In this video you can learn 50 very common and useful Korean words with examples from many different Korean dramas. Note that most of these words and expressions are informal and would normally only be used with people you are familiar with like friends or family, but they are used very often and you will hear them frequently in everyday life in Korean. Also be aware that Korean and English are very different languages and it is not always easy to directly translate the meaning or pronunciation of a Korean word.

For example, in Korean the "L" is often pronounced closer to an English rolling "R". Also, when a Korean word starts with an "M" as in for instance [mwo] or [molla], the "M" is denasalized in Korean making it sound closer to an English "B". It's comparable to saying "mwo" while pinching your nose or having a cold!


Check out my channel for lots more! :)

title: ūüďļ‚ú®Learn Korean Phrases with K-Drama ŪěėŽāī [him-nae] (Formal: ŪěėŽāīžöĒ / ŪěėŽāīžĄłžöĒ)
description:
Today's expression is "ŪěėŽāī," which means "Cheer up!" or "Stay strong!" in English. This phrase is commonly used to encourage someone who is facing a challenge or going through a tough time. It‚Äôs an expression of support, reminding someone to keep going.

ūüďĚ Breakdown 
Ūěė [him]: strength, energy 
ŽāīŽč§ [nae-da]: to make, to produce

‚ö†ÔłŹ The difference between ŪôĒžĚīŪĆÖ and ŪěėŽāī
"ŪôĒžĚīŪĆÖ" and "ŪěėŽāī" both mean "cheer up," but they‚Äôre used a bit differently. "ŪôĒžĚīŪĆÖ" is like saying "You can do it!" and is used to cheer someone on before a challenge, such as an exam or a sports game. It‚Äôs upbeat and full of energy. On the other hand, "ŪěėŽāī" is closer to "Stay strong!" and is used to support someone going through a tough time. It‚Äôs a bit more comforting and often conveys encouragement during difficult situations.

"Learning Korean Through Drama: 'ž≤úž≤úŪěą Ž®ĻžĖī' - Take Your Time Eating"
description:
In this video, we'll dive into the phrase "ž≤úž≤úŪěą Ž®ĻžĖī", which means "Take your time eating," as heard in the popular Korean drama "ŽąąŽ¨ľžĚė žó¨žôē." This expression is often used to remind someone to eat slowly and enjoy their meal. Along with this, we‚Äôll break down similar phrases related to eating and introduce you to common dining expressions in Korean.



Breaking Down the Expression ūüďĚ:
"ž≤úž≤úŪěą Ž®ĻžĖī" - Take your time eating

"ž≤úž≤úŪěą" means "slowly" and is commonly used to encourage someone to take their time, whether it‚Äôs eating, walking, or doing an activity.
"Ž®ĻžĖī" comes from "Ž®ĻŽč§", which means "to eat." Adding "žĖī" at the end creates a casual command to eat. Combined, the phrase gently reminds someone to eat at a leisurely pace.
"ŽßõžěąžĖī?" - Is it delicious?
"ŽßõžěąžĖī" comes from "ŽßõžěąŽč§", meaning "delicious." It's a casual way to ask if someone is enjoying their food.


Example Sentences ūüďö:
"ž≤úž≤úŪěą Ž®ĻžĖī. žčúÍįĄžĚī ŽßéžēĄ."
Translation: "Take your time eating. We have plenty of time."
Usage: Use this when encouraging someone to relax and enjoy their meal.
"ŽßõžěąžĖī? ŽćĒ Ž®ĻžĖī."
Translation: "Is it delicious? Have some more."
Usage: This is a typical way to ask someone if they like their meal and offer more food.

Related Expressions:
"ž≤úž≤úŪěą Ūēī." - Take your time (with something)
This can be used in any context where you want to tell someone to do something slowly, not just eating.
"ŽßéžĚī Ž®ĻžĖī." - Eat a lot
A friendly way to encourage someone to eat plenty, often heard at meals in Korea.

Cultural Insight ūüĆć:
In Korea, meals are a time to connect and enjoy food together. It‚Äôs common to hear expressions like "ž≤úž≤úŪěą Ž®ĻžĖī" or "ŽßéžĚī Ž®ĻžĖī" as a way to show care for others during shared meals. Encouraging someone to eat slowly and enjoy their food is a sign of hospitality.

Practice Makes Perfect ūüí™:
Try using "ž≤úž≤úŪěą Ž®ĻžĖī" or "ŽßõžěąžĖī?" in your conversations to get comfortable with common dining phrases in Korean. Practice these phrases to sound more natural during meals with friends or family.

description:

A big thank you to Kia EV9 for sponsoring this video!

This isn't an ad for the restaurant but let's support local businesses!
Kim Kyeong Ja Wonjo Son Kalguksu Bossam:

Huge thank you to Hashtag United for giving us their time and letting us film. Go check them out here:
https://www.youtube.com/user/spencerbets

We got the original chicken and sauces from Itaewon Burger and Chicken and the spicy Volcano sauce from Pelicana.

title: We had dinner with BLACKPINK at Buckingham Palace
description:
Today we had one of the most surreal experiences in our lives. We were invited back to the Buckingham Palace to dine with the King, and the royal family, the Korean President, Black Pink, president of Samsung, president of LG, and a lot of VIPS from the UK and Korea. It was such an honour to take part in this wonderful friendship between the two countries we love. We genuinely couldn't have done any of this without you guys' support, so thank you all! 

--

title: British College Students try Korean Chicken BBQ + Fried Rice rolls!
description:
Today we take the boys on a road trip to Gangwon State! There are so many beautiful regions in Korea and it‚Äôd be a shame not to make the most of the time we have with the boys in Korea! So we thought we‚Äôd introduce them to a part of a Korean uni culture called ‚ÄúMT‚ÄĚ and show them around beautiful cities in Gangwon State while we‚Äôre at it! First stop: good food/ chicken barbecue!!

title: British College Students get SHY in front of K-POP Girl Group fromis_9!!
description:
Straight from the military, we take the boys through a rather extreme change of environment: KPOP Headquarters. We meet with Korean soldiers‚Äô favourite girl group, fromis_9 to learn their dance (and see if any of us can join their group to form fromis_10)! Watch the video to find out who was the best and worst! We then take the boys for a relaxing evening at a Korean cinema, trying all types of Korean cinema snacks and watching a film on ScreenX (https://bit.ly/ScreenX_Eng)! Hope you enjoy today‚Äôs episode as well!

--

title: British Highschoolers Fly to Korea to try REAL Street Food!!
description:
A year after a handshake and a promise‚Ä¶ the British Highschoolers have finally come on a School Trip to Korea! 7 students. 3 teachers. Months of planning with our whole crew. And a trip full of life-changing, unforgettable new experiences. Friendship, bonding, and young men experiencing and then falling in love with a whole new culture.
We hope you enjoy the whole series - stay tuned, there's more to come!!!

Special thanks to Masung Tteokbokki for sponsoring this episode and helping us to make this trip as special as possible!
http://www.masungfood.com/

--

We opened a Korean Street Toast Restaurant in London!!
We‚Äôre launching our Korean Street Toast kiosk in Granary Square, Kings Cross this Friday! Follow @KoreanToastLondon on instagram for all the info. http://instagram.com/koreantoastlondon

Go check out the YouTubers who came to try our toast here:
Sorted Food: www.youtube.com/SORTEDFood
Tom Scott: https://www.youtube.com/TomScottGo
Taz: www.youtube.com/ClickForTaz

They all make brilliant content so go show them some love!
Huge thanks to  @ÍĶ≠ÍįÄŽĻĄ Gabiekook for helping us develop the perfect street toast menu, and to Alin and Kate for supporting us and helping make this dream a reality!

Alin and Kate share our kimchi-obsession, and our desire to share it world-wide - so they developed the kimchi we used in our menu specifically to be as accessible as possible - perfect one-portion sizes without having to worry about storing and stinking out the rest of your fridge!
Go check out Picky Wicky Kimchi here: https://www.pickywicky.com

And thanks to Jang Hot Sauce (@janghotsauce)  for sponsoring our restaurant! We wanted an authentic Korean spice in our spicy toast, and Jang Hot Sauce has a delicious traditional Korean Gochujang base. Perfect!
Go check out Jang Hot Sauce here: https://bit.ly/3l3OWSV

All of the crew had a negative COVID-19 test prior to shooting.
All activities were fully risk assessed and monitored in accordance with UK government guidelines.

title: British Uni Students get a job at a Korean Convenience Store!
description:
After trying almost every snack they sell, we surprised Armand and Max with the news that like a true student they are going to get a part time job at a Korean Convenience Store too!!

If you're in Korean, find a photosim booth and take a photo with us using the Korean Englishman photo-frame. We would love to see all the photos so do tag us on instagram!

This video isn't sponsored but thank you to GS25 for helping us film this episode!

--

žó¨žĚėŽŹĄ žÉĚÍ≥†Íłį
žĄúžöł žėĀŽďĪŪŹ¨ÍĶ¨ žĚėžā¨ŽčĻŽĆÄŽ°ú1Íłł 34

Today we meet the legendary Englishman, our childhood hero, David Beckham and treat him to top quality Korean beef!! We picked up the meat from the best Hanwoo restaurant we know in Seoul and took it to him! We still can‚Äôt believe this actually happened! I was so nervous I forgot to introduce him to our favourite combination to eat with beef until David eyed it up and asked me about it! ūüėā It was such an honour to meet him and film a mukbang with him, and we really hope you guys enjoy this episode!!

Also a huge thank you to the manager at "Yeoeuido Saenggogi" for preparing the Hanwoo for us!
Address: 34 Uisadang-daero 1-gil, Yeoeuido-dong, Seoul

Today we‚Äôre back at the school, this time to take a look at the notorious Korean SAT. Huge thanks to all the boys who were willing to put themselves in someone else‚Äôs shoes today and try this exam!!
We‚Äôve done a few videos about this huge exam in Korea before, and every year I think it‚Äôs a great opportunity to remember what so many people are going through right now.
For everyone in Korea about to take the KSAT next week we want to send love and support from the whole KoreanEnglishman and JOLLY team. We‚Äôll be praying for you!

Everyone in this video including the crew had a negative COVID-19 test prior to shooting.
All activities were fully risk assessed and monitored in accordance with UK government guidelines.

moment when they find out they are sponsored by the drink they fell in love with since day one -  Milkis!

title: This is the #1 Korean BBQ in the World.
description:
Today we go to the restaurant that has just won "Best Korean BBQ" 1st place for the second year running, to taste the best of the best of Korean Hanwoo BBQ.

Hanwoo has been called "the forbidden beef", because it is impossible to get anywhere outside of Korea. Arguably the highest quality Beef in the world, we flew our friend Mr Z, an Italian Chef, all the way to Korea to try it.

This is the first episode of a 6 past series - searching out the very best food Korea has to offer.

This is not a sponsor but thank you to Chef Min Kyeong Hwan and to Born & Bred for giving us an amazing experience!
--

title: British Highschoolers try GIANT Korean Beef Ribs!!
description:
After a long day in the life of a Korean High Schooler, Nati and Harvey came out on top! They used the time we gave them to study the hardest and learn the most Korean, and as a reward we took them to maybe my favourite restaurant in Korea: Mongtan. Mongtan is a restaurant that I‚Äôve previously waited hours just to try and get a reservation, and they do a unique take on traditional Korean BBQ; smoking and then BBQing giant beef ribs to perfection!
This video isn‚Äôt sponsored, but thanks to Mongtan for letting us film, and congratulations to the boys for learning Korean so well!

--

title: British Rappers try the ULTIMATE Korean Pork BBQ FEAST!!
description:
Today is the last episode with the British Rappers. We wanted to come full circle and end on a special Korean BBQ meal but this time with Korea's favourite meat, PORK!

Thank you to everyone who enjoyed our British Rappers in Korea series!

And a big thank you to our series sponsor Original Beer Company (OBC) for helping us make this content.

7 years ago you guys in Korea sent us a brand new instant noodle to try: Fire Noodles. We filmed a video giving them to our friends, and little did we know that would be the start of an international viral challenge. So today, we‚Äôre going back to our roots‚Ä¶

If you want to see us take the boys to Korea someday then hit like on the video and comment below! 

All of the crew had a negative COVID-19 test prior to shooting.
All activities were fully risk assessed and monitored in accordance with UK government guidelines.

We‚Äôve done some pretty special celeb interviews, often introducing celebrities visiting Korea to Korean food for the first time (https://youtu.be/DQWUAb-hmlg), but one of our favourites was this interview with KPOP stars Mark and Jaehyun from NCT. Here‚Äôs the first: our full interview with NCT uncut (basically!) and fully subtitled. 

#NCT127 #NCT #SuperM

The next one will be uploaded this Saturday, so stay tuned!
Enjoy.

title: Kingsman Actors try Korean Fried Chicken
F1 Driver eats Bodyweight in Korean BBQ!! Ft. Alex Albon
description:
A few months ago we met the Thai British F1 driver Alex Albon at an event and invited him to go out and try some Korean food. Alex is one of only 20 Formula One drivers in the world, and is ranked amongst the very best drivers on the grid. He‚Äôs driven for Toro Rosso, Red Bull and most recently for Williams. During the mid-season break he took time out of his busy schedule to meet us at one of my favourite London BBQ spots for some food and a chat.

Thank you to Alex and his team for giving their time to film!
All the best for the Singapore Grand Prix!

Today's video is not sponsored, but let's support local businesses!
You can find : Chung'dam London, 35-36 Greek St, London W1D 5DL

--

And after the last video we knew that we introduce them to proper Korean chicken and beer‚Ä¶ but although there are some great options in the UK we wanted to give them a really authentic experience!

title: Korean Navy Officers Try British Rations for the First Time!!
description:
In our last video we had the Korean navy officer introduce Korean navy rations to British Uni boys. And today we introduce British rations to Korean navy officers! It was an amazing cultural exchange and very interesting to see what Korean officers thought of the variety of courses in British army rations! What was their favourite part of the meal? Watch till the end to find out!:)

The twins have had tasted Korean BBQ, they've even had a taste of Korean chicken... And while they would very happily eat only those two things for the rest of their trip, I really wanted to show them some truly Korean style food. Something more traditional, but still done in a way that these twins, with the simple tastes of elementary school kids, would still enjoy. 

Big thanks to Chef Lee, our very own #strongcoolguy. This episode wasn't sponsored but they took great care of us and the food was fantastic.

Benedict Cumberbatch tries Kimbap for the first time, Pom Klementieff is mind blown by Korean pears, Tom Holland falls in love with Korean Banana Milk and Tom Hiddleston may have the smoothest voice in history. This week we have the privilege of filming with 4 members of the cast of Avengers Infinity War!!

With a busy schedule of press interviews in their hotel, we wanted to give them all a unique, fun Korean experience during their stay. So we put together a set and chose a variety of homely Korean food that we know foreigners love from our previous videos, that also fit the actors' dietary requirements. Kimbap from two of my favourite Kimbap joints in Seoul, the sort of Kimbap bursting with fillings and flavour, that makes me question how on earth Kimbap isn't known around the world. And fresh juicy Korean fruit that is unlike anything we have in the UK. 

This is Avengers trying Korean food!
